concepts.name: Exponential Growth & Decay
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a quantity grows or shrinks in proportion to its current size — compound interest, bacteria, radioactive decay — it traces the exponential curve.
concepts.formal_gloss: The solution y(t) = y_0 e^{kt} of the linear ODE dy/dt = ky, growing without bound for k > 0 and decaying to zero for k < 0.
sources: AP Calculus / MIT OCW 18.03 opening unit
Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, ch. 2 (flows on the line)

concepts.name: Slope Field
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw a tiny arrow at every point showing which way the rule pushes; solutions are the paths that go with the flow of the arrows.
concepts.formal_gloss: A plot assigning to each point (t, y) the slope f(t, y) prescribed by y' = f(t, y); solution curves are exactly the curves everywhere tangent to the field.

concepts.name: Separation of Variables
concepts.intuition_gloss: Herd all the y's to one side of the equation and all the t's to the other, then integrate each side separately to unlock the solution.
concepts.formal_gloss: A method solving y' = g(t)h(y) by rewriting it as dy/h(y) = g(t) dt and integrating both sides; the same divide-and-conquer idea later splits PDEs into products of one-variable problems.

concepts.name: Initial Value Problem & Existence-Uniqueness
concepts.intuition_gloss: Given the rule of change plus where you start, there is exactly one future — the theorem that makes determinism mathematically honest.
concepts.formal_gloss: An ODE y' = f(t, y) together with a condition y(t_0) = y_0; the Picard–Lindelöf theorem guarantees a unique local solution when f is continuous and Lipschitz in y.

concepts.name: Linear ODEs & Superposition
concepts.intuition_gloss: For linear rules of change, solutions add: find a few exponential building blocks and every solution is a mix of them.
concepts.formal_gloss: An ODE of the form y^(n) + a_{n-1}(t)y^(n-1) + ... + a_0(t)y = g(t); homogeneous solutions form a vector space, and constant-coefficient cases are solved by the roots of the characteristic polynomial via y = e^{rt}.

concepts.name: Harmonic Oscillator
concepts.intuition_gloss: A weight bobbing on a spring — the universal model of anything that wobbles around a resting point, from bridges to molecules to circuits.
concepts.formal_gloss: The linear second-order ODE x'' + 2ζω x' + ω²x = F(t), with sinusoidal solutions, exponentially damped envelopes, and resonance under periodic forcing near ω.

concepts.name: Systems of ODEs & the Matrix Exponential
concepts.intuition_gloss: When several changing quantities push on each other, bundle them into one vector and let a matrix run the whole dance at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: A vector ODE x' = f(x) on R^n; the linear case x' = Ax is solved by x(t) = e^{At}x_0, with growth, decay, rotation, and shear classified by the eigenvalues of A.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — How (and why) to raise e to the power of a matrix

concepts.name: Phase Space & Phase Portrait
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of graphing position against time, plot the system's full state as a single moving point — its whole life becomes one curve in a landscape.
concepts.formal_gloss: The decomposition of state space into the orbits of a dynamical system, depicting equilibria, closed orbits, and the geometry of trajectories at a glance.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

sources: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, ch. 5–6

concepts.name: Dynamical System & Flow
concepts.intuition_gloss: Freeze the rule, and time itself becomes a machine: feed in a state and a duration, get out the future state.
concepts.formal_gloss: A one-parameter family of maps φ_t on a state space with φ_0 = id and φ_{s+t} = φ_s ∘ φ_t, generated in the smooth case by integrating a vector field.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Equilibrium Point
concepts.intuition_gloss: A state where the pushing stops — if the system lands exactly there, it stays forever.
concepts.formal_gloss: A point x* with f(x*) = 0 for the flow x' = f(x), or F(x*) = x* for a map — a constant orbit of the system.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Stability & Lyapunov Functions
concepts.intuition_gloss: Nudge a marble in a bowl and it settles back; nudge it on a dome and it flees — stability asks which kind of resting point you have.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equilibrium is Lyapunov stable if nearby trajectories stay nearby and asymptotically stable if they also converge; a function decreasing along trajectories (a Lyapunov function) certifies stability without solving the equations.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Linearization & Hyperbolicity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Zoom in on a resting point until the curved dynamics look straight, then read the local fate from a matrix's eigenvalues.
concepts.formal_gloss: Approximating x' = f(x) near an equilibrium x* by x' = Df(x*)(x − x*); the Hartman–Grobman theorem says the true phase portrait is locally conjugate to this linear one whenever no eigenvalue has zero real part.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Limit Cycle
concepts.intuition_gloss: An isolated closed loop the system settles into — the mathematical heartbeat behind clocks, neurons, and predator-prey cycles.
concepts.formal_gloss: An isolated closed orbit of a flow; in the plane, the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem guarantees one inside any trapping region containing no equilibria.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Bifurcation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Turn a dial slowly and the system's whole repertoire can suddenly reorganize — new resting points appear from nowhere, or a steady state bursts into oscillation.
concepts.formal_gloss: A parameter value at which the topological structure of a system's phase portrait changes, classified locally into normal forms such as saddle-node, transcritical, pitchfork, and Hopf.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Iterated Maps (Discrete Dynamics)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Apply the same rule again and again — tomorrow's value computed from today's — and watch where the repetition leads.
concepts.formal_gloss: A dynamical system generated by iterating a map F: X → X, with orbits x_{n+1} = F(x_n); fixed points and cycles are stable when the derivative of the (composed) map has magnitude below 1.

resources.title: Complexity Explorer — Introduction to Dynamical Systems and Chaos (Feldman)

sources: Devaney, An Introduction to Chaotic Dynamical Systems

concepts.name: Logistic Map & Period Doubling
concepts.intuition_gloss: A one-line population rule that, as you turn up the growth knob, goes from calm to oscillating to full-blown chaos — the fruit fly of chaos theory.
concepts.formal_gloss: The map x_{n+1} = r x_n(1 − x_n) on [0,1], whose attracting cycles undergo a period-doubling cascade to chaos governed by the universal Feigenbaum constant δ ≈ 4.669.

resources.title: Veritasium — This equation will change how you see the world (the logistic map)

concepts.name: Chaos & Sensitive Dependence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A perfectly deterministic system whose future is still unforecastable, because today's tiniest rounding error snowballs exponentially.
concepts.formal_gloss: Aperiodic long-term behavior in a deterministic system exhibiting sensitive dependence on initial conditions, typically together with dense periodic orbits and topological mixing.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Lyapunov Exponent
concepts.intuition_gloss: The speedometer of chaos — how fast two almost-identical starting points fly apart, measured in doublings per unit time.
concepts.formal_gloss: The asymptotic exponential growth rate λ = lim_{t→∞} (1/t) ln(|δx(t)|/|δx(0)|) of infinitesimal perturbations; a positive largest exponent is the standard quantitative signature of chaos.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Attractors & Strange Attractors
concepts.intuition_gloss: The shape a system's long-run behavior settles onto — a point, a loop, or in chaotic systems an infinitely detailed butterfly-shaped filigree.
concepts.formal_gloss: A compact invariant set that attracts all orbits from a neighborhood; strange attractors carry fractal structure and chaotic dynamics, the Lorenz system being the canonical example.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

concepts.name: Poincaré Map
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of watching the whole orbit, strobe-light it each time it crosses a chosen screen — turning a continuous tangle into a simple sequence of dots.
concepts.formal_gloss: The first-return map induced by a flow on a transversal section, converting a continuous system into a discrete map one dimension lower; periodic orbits of the flow become fixed points of the map.

resources.title: Strogatz — Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (Cornell lectures)

sources: Poincaré's three-body work (historical root of the field)

concepts.name: Hamiltonian & Conservative Systems
concepts.intuition_gloss: Frictionless physics as geometry: energy is a landscape, and motion follows its contour lines, never compressing the cloud of possible states.
concepts.formal_gloss: Dynamics q' = ∂H/∂p, p' = −∂H/∂q generated by a Hamiltonian function H; the flow conserves H and preserves phase-space volume (Liouville's theorem).

resources.title: Susskind — The Theoretical Minimum: Classical Mechanics

sources: Arnold, Mathematical Methods of Classical Mechanics

concepts.name: Ergodic Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: For thoroughly mixing systems, following one trajectory long enough tells you the same story as sampling everywhere at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of measure-preserving transformations; Birkhoff's theorem states that for ergodic systems, time averages along almost every orbit equal the space average against the invariant measure.
sources: Walters, An Introduction to Ergodic Theory

concepts.name: Partial Differential Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a quantity varies across space and time — heat in a rod, waves on water — its law of change relates rates in several directions at once.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation relating an unknown function of several variables to its partial derivatives; second-order linear PDEs are classified as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic, each with its own theory.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — But what is a partial differential equation?

concepts.name: Heat Equation (Diffusion)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Sharp temperature differences smooth themselves out — each point drifts toward the average of its neighbors.
concepts.formal_gloss: The parabolic PDE u_t = α∇²u, solved on bounded domains by separation of variables and Fourier series, with instant smoothing and maximum-principle behavior.
sources: Fourier's Théorie analytique de la chaleur (historical root)

concepts.name: Wave Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Pluck a string and the disturbance travels: its shape persists while its position moves, at a speed set by the medium.
concepts.formal_gloss: The hyperbolic PDE u_tt = c²∇²u, with traveling-wave solutions f(x − ct) + g(x + ct) by d'Alembert's formula and standing-wave normal modes on bounded domains.

concepts.name: Numerical Solution of ODEs
concepts.intuition_gloss: When formulas fail, take tiny time-steps: nudge the state along the arrows over and over and let a computer trace the trajectory.
concepts.formal_gloss: Discrete-time approximation schemes x_{n+1} = x_n + h·Φ(x_n, h) for initial value problems, from first-order Euler to the fourth-order Runge–Kutta family, with accuracy and stability governed by the step size and the method's order.

concepts.name: Population & Interaction Models
concepts.intuition_gloss: Simple rules — grow, crowd, hunt, infect — turned into equations that reproduce booms, crashes, cycles, and epidemic waves.
concepts.formal_gloss: Nonlinear ODE models such as logistic growth y' = ry(1 − y/K), the Lotka–Volterra predator-prey system, and SIR compartmental models, analyzed through their equilibria and phase portraits.

concepts.name: Stochastic Differential Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: A rule of change with random jitter built in — the path of a pollen grain buffeted by molecules, or a stock price wandering upward.
concepts.formal_gloss: An equation dX_t = μ(X_t)dt + σ(X_t)dW_t driven by Brownian motion, given rigorous meaning by the Itô (or Stratonovich) stochastic integral.
